speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Heilman, in Birkenau, did friendships develop among the prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I-- indeed they did. I think that everybody who lost a family needed to replace it. Indeed, if you did not have a friend, you did not have a zeal for life. I think that friendships and taking care of somebody other than yourself made a great deal of difference. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Perhaps I could go back to the Lager B when we were transferred [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to Lager A, which used to be men's lager before. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were told that we should volunteer for a-- as a metallurgist. We didn't know what metallurgists were, but we decided we're going to be metallurgists. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's 'we'?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister and I. And as metallurgists, we were-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] we were then scheduled to work. What we didn't know but eventually knew-- that was a munition factory. In the transfer from Lager A to Lager B, we were in a block whose Blockalteste, or responsible for the block, was a non-Jewish woman by the name of Maria, who eventually became a kapo of the camp. At that time, my sister became sick with typhoid. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was a kind of a hospital in the camp. But anybody who was in the hospital was doomed and was taken to the gas right away. So we decided not to let her go to the hospital. And it so happened that there was a market in Birkenau. People, particularly the German prisoners, used to get parcels from home. And after working between two blocks, they used to sell the goodies from their parcels for bread or soup. Since my sister is so very sick-- was so very sick, and she couldn't eat either the bread nor the soup, I used to run to the market and get her, you know, sometimes an apple and sometimes a little bit of sugar, and whatever I could find. And the Blockalteste was very, very kind to her. The roll calls were going on for hours and hours and hours. And she used to keep her in the block for the last-- till the last minute and let her go out only when necessary. And in this way, she helped her get over the illness. From that block, we were transferred to another block. I believe it was block number eight, where the union workers were. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Before-- the union factory was just being built. It wasn't ready yet. In the meantime, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] everybody that was in a Kommando, or in a special department, used to go in the morning for work. And those that weren't assigned stayed in the block. However, from time to time, they needed people to work around the camp to do whatever cleaning there was to be done. And nobody wanted to work, so we used to run away. And I used to hide behind the door-- the front door of the block [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] under a little window where the bread was being stored. And the woman that was responsible for that was always chasing me out of there because they were responsible for the quota of workers to give them. And I hated them with a passionate hatred. This became my very good friend, Marta, whom we got to know. She was from Slovakia. We became very, very friendly. And eventually, she saved my life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My sister was still weak from the sickness. And through the offices of Marta, she was given a job, which was to guard the door at night. And for that, she-- my sister received then a payment-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a cup of jam. So we were very lucky. We had jam. And so we ran to the market-- there was another market from the girls who were working in quote, 'Kanada.' This Kommando was called because Canada was supposed to be the richest country in the world. And these girls were working in sorting of the effects from the transport. And they used to smuggle into the camp anything you can dream of, from silk stockings to silk underwear to, you know, whatever people use to take with them and they managed to smuggle into. Anyway, we went to the market with our cup of jam and with a spoon. And for three spoons of jam, we got our first toothbrush. And then we couldn't understand how come the whole cup was only three spoons. And then we became wise in commerce. What you do, you choose a smaller spoon, and you dilute the jam with water. This way, you could get another toothbrush and two bras. So those were the first purchases that we made. And we became friendly with Marta. It was friendship that lasts until today.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about news from the outside? Did you know what was going on with the war effort?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was another thing that happened. There was a group of girls that used to get together, tell stories about each other, sing Hebrew songs, dream of Israel. And somehow, we lifted ourselves from the reality of the camp into the fantasy of our meetings. And they became more real to us than the reality around us. In this--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you able to meet? How were you able to meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: After work, you could go to each other's block. There was a period before the curfew that you could go and visit each other. And we used to meet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were also news. There were girls-- there were, in this group, actually, there were Jewish girls and non-Jewish girls, as well. I remember there was a Yugoslavian girl among us. And she seemed to have the news from outside. There was-- there was always a way to know. We knew what was going on outside. We knew, certainly, when the Germans were beginning to lose the war. And this was, of course, for us, great news.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we knew what was going on. And in this particular group, we also learned that the war is coming to an end. This had to be in beginning of '44.

speaker: interviewee
text: We learned that partisan-- Polish partisans from the outside and the Underground in the inside are preparing a revolt, and that the Germans are-- when the Germans are losing, they are going to rebel against it. And we knew about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in this particular group, we started to think, well, what can we do in order to help? And what we did was, we secured keys from the agricultural tools [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] storeroom so that we could take rakes and hoes and whatever to, to help ourselves. And there's-- we also decided that, you know, we can-- we have access to the gunpowder, and we can start smuggling the gunpowder. So what were you-- what was your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were working-- at this time, we were already working in the munition factory. My sister was working in the [NON-ENGLISH], which was the gunpowder room. Their job was to fill a certain part of whatever it is that was manufactured there with the gunpowder. There were only six girls working in this gunpowder room. I was working two department away from her in a little-- in a big room. But my job was to measure with a precision instrument a certain part of bakelite, which was called [NON-ENGLISH], for a specific indentation in it. This was my job.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the smuggling going on? What was the route of the gunpowder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was very curious. And although we were not allowed to get up from our seat and go to the bathroom-- only with permission, after a while, you got accustomed to the place. And I used to go around the factory, just to look around at what was going on. I used to take two little metal boxes that were used for garbage and walk around as if I was, you know, doing something. And eventually, I used to take these two boxes to my sister. She used to put a little bit of gunpowder, wrapped up in a little rag tied with a string, into a box and put garbage on the top box. And I was walking with these two boxes from my place to her door, and from her door back into my place, and put it under the table and put it inside the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cuff of my dress because we didn't have pockets. From there, I used to go to the washroom and in the washroom, share this gunpowder with another girl, with Ella. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the way from the factory-- it was about three kilometers to Birkenau where we lived-- we used to carry this on our bodies. From time to time, there were searches. When we heard that there was a search, we used to unwrap this gunpowder, throw it on the ground, and mix it with our feet on the ground so it was not distinguishable from the dirt underfoot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if there was no search?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we used to bring it to Birkenau. I'd give it to my sister. And my sister gave it to-- I don't know. Either directly to Roza Robota or to somebody else-- I don't know exactly who gave it to Roza Robota.

speaker: interviewer
text: And from Roza Robota, it went--

speaker: interviewee
text: It went to a special hiding place. Roza Robota had contact with the men from the crematorium. And they had approval letters to come into the women's camp. And in that particular spot, they used to come and pick it up and bring it into the crematorium. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In October '44, there was a revolt in the crematorium. The Sonderkommandos-- those were the people who were manning the crematorium-- knew that from time to time, after a certain period of work, they were being murdered, as not to bear witnesses. And that particular group decided that they are going to rebel. They used this gunpowder and manufactured little hand grenades made out of metal around boxes of shoe polish with a wick and filled with gunpowder. And when you lit it, it exploded. I don't know how much damage it did or it didn't. This revolt-- it took place on October 7, 1944-- was aborted. Either they were betrayed or whatever. All the Sonderkommando people were killed. But the crematorium was destroyed as well. So there were four crematoriums in Birkenau, and one was destroyed by this. After the revolt, the Germans found these little handmade grenades and they identified the gunpowder, which, of course, we didn't know that gunpowder had some kind of special characteristic. They identified this gunpowder as coming only from the union and the only from [GERMAN], where my sister worked. They [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] started an investigation. They imprisoned four girls-- Roza Robota, Ella Gartner, Regina Safirsztain, and Ester Wajcblum. They tortured them mercilessly and eventually hanged them publicly on January 5, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you witness your sister's hanging?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] During the period of my sister being incarcerated in the bunker which was a prison within the prison, Marta succeeded to bring me letters from her and from Jakob, who was the kapo of this bunker. He was very, very sympathetic to the girls. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: He was sympathetic to the girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: And through him, I received letters-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Hello?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying that Marta brought letters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marta brought, brought letters. And she was trying to get me to see my sister. But it never materialized. But Marta, sawing my depression, arranged with bribes to put me in a hospital in Auschwitz. At this point, the union commander was in Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as she was trying to reassure me and tell me-- apparently Jakob [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] received an order to execute the girls. But he was stalling for time. And he refused. He had the power to refuse it. And he said he's not going to execute all the order and-- unless he's going to get it from Berlin. So it lasted for a while. And finally the order from Berlin came, and he didn't have a choice. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Marta was always coming and visiting me every day. And she was telling me that there was no order, that they are going-- that everything is going to be OK. And she wouldn't tell me the truth. On the day of the execution, I was in the hospital. And Marta came to take me out. The union commander was released earlier to witness the execution. But Marta's friends, [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH], grabbed me and put me in the block, so that I wouldn't have to witness it. And I didn't know what was going on. I suspected, but I wasn't sure. And when we were on the block and I wanted to run out, they bodily held me. They wouldn't let me out. And I heard this collective groan. And I knew what happened. So I wasn't-- I didn't witness it with my eyes, but I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the last words your sister left you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I don't remember it by heart. But I have it written, her last letter that I got through Marta, in which she is saying [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] that all hope is gone, that through her window of the cell, she can see the feet of the prisoners that are coming back from work, that all the hated sounds of the camp life-- the screaming for the soup and for the tea that was so hateful to her-- were so dear to her now, that all hope is gone, and that she so much wants to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get through all that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lost my mind immediately after that. The execution was on January 5th. The evacuation of Auschwitz was on January 18, 1945. When--

speaker: interviewee
text: Five. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The Russians liberated Auschwitz on January 19-27, but the Germans evacuated Auschwitz on January 18, when we went on the death march. I didn't want to move from Auschwitz. I really didn't care what was going on around me. And Marta promised Ester that she is going to take care of me. And she bodily took me from Auschwitz. And we were walking. See, it was the death march.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that point, I was 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister? When she was executed, how old was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, she was just barely 20. Her birthday was on January 16. She was executed on January 5th. Some people, according to the calendarium of Auschwitz claim it was January 6th. Marta and I remember it on January 5th-- whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the death march?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was totally out of my mind. I didn't know what was going on. And I was fascinated with the beauty of it. It was very cold. It was snowing, and it was beautiful. It was just like a Christmas picture-postcard kind of a view, all white with the little houses, with their beautiful trees. And we're just walking. The people that were guarding us were the Wehrmacht soldiers at this point, who were unfit to serve at the front. So they were walking us. But they were old and weak, and they couldn't walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we used to help them. What they wanted was walking with us. I remember we used to help him walk because he couldn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We used to sleep in-- with the cows in barns. And we didn't have any food. So we used to steal the cow's food, which was turnips. And we'd, we'd feed those turnips until a certain point when we were taken by a train to Ravensbruck, which was in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the way, during the march, we could have easily escaped. But with-- there was nowhere to go. So we-- so we continued walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet any of the local population?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not that I remember of-- now. It wasn't so good for everybody. There were other people who were walking with soldiers with dogs. And any stragglers were executed on the spot. But you know, I was just out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened in Ravensbruck?

speaker: interviewee
text: At Ravensbruck? We were there, I think, only overnight. And from there, we were transported by train to Neustadt-Glewe, which, I believe, is near Hamburg somewhere. And this was a little camp where we were liberated eventually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the liberators?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that the Russians and the British met there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did liberation mean for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. I was very sick. I remember we were in the camp. And there was nobody around. And we're sitting there. In Auschwitz, we were afraid to stay because we were told that Auschwitz was mined, and it will explode, that the Germans would explode the camp. So this is why we went. In Neustadt-Glewe, we didn't know anything. And there was-- I was very sick, both mentally and physically. I was rotten from the waist down with boils. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so-- when there was nobody around, finally-- we were sitting and sitting, and finally, some brave girls thought to go out. There was nobody around. So they raided the storerooms with food. There was powdered milk and powdered eggs. And they simply started to gorge themselves. And unfortunately, many of them died with overeating. I was too sick to go. So maybe this is why I survived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Near our camp was a camp for SS men. And Marta and I got in there. And we got a hot shower. There was still hot water in this SS men's room. So the first thing we did was take a hot shower in this room. Then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were transported into the town of Neustadt-Glewe. And very quickly, there were repatriation process. Next to me in union factory was a Belgian girl who was telling us how good the chocolate and ice cream was in Belgium.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Marta wanted to go back to Slovakia to try and find her family. And I said to her, come on, let's go and try the ice cream first. And as a result, we went to Belgium. And both of us were in Belgium after liberation. How long did you stay in Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: For about a year. Marta stayed in Belgium, and I immigrated to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you able to rebuild your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: While I was in Belgium, we were sent to a-- by the Jewish community to a kind of a summer camp. And in this camp, I was writing a diary. And I wrote whatever I could remember up until the execution. I think that writing this diary helped me [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] come to terms. I left the life behind, the, the camp life, and started life again. Mind you, I was very, very depressed for a long time before I started to rebuild it. I found the loss of Marta as the ultimate loss. It was somebody from the past. I was very young. I was 16 at this point. And I took it badly.

speaker: interviewer
text: The loss of--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marta-- in a sense, because she got married and stayed in Belgium when I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Went to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: --went to Israel. In Israel, in 1947, I met my husband. We married, had two children, went back to school, and lived ever after.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and you studied?

speaker: interviewee
text: Social work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And have you worked in that profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked in this profession for-- in Israel, in Boston, and in Canada for 30 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever find out news about any other members of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- when I came to Israel, I was reunited with my sister, who left in 1939. She lives there with-- now, her husband. And we were reunited in Israel.
